w:r.w:t: Full Project Guide • 12-Day Solo Build Plan

GORO is a production-grade Distributed Task Queue built in Go. It lets applications offload background work (emails, image processing, webhooks) to a pool of concurrent workers. Unlike tutorial queues that are simple FIFO lists, GORO adds priority scheduling, smart retry logic, a Dead Letter Queue, live Prometheus metrics, and a real-time web dashboard — all features you would find in tools like Sidekiq, Celery, or Asynq.
This project is intentionally scoped for a 12-day solo build. Every feature chosen maximises resume impact while staying realistic for one developer.
What Makes This Different from Other Queue Projects
Priority queue with 4 levels (critical / high / default / low) — not just FIFO
Exponential backoff retry + Dead Letter Queue for failed jobs
Live WebSocket dashboard served directly by the Go server
Three real demo job handlers (email, image resize, webhook) so recruiters see real use cases
Docker Compose + GitHub Actions CI — shows production mindset
— any service that creates a job and pushes it to the queue.
— a Redis sorted set / list that holds pending jobs, ordered by priority.
— N goroutines that pull jobs from the queue and execute them concurrently.
— a separate Redis list where jobs go after exhausting all retries.
— a Go function registered to handle a specific job type (e.g. "send_email").
The system is split into two independently runnable binaries: the API server and the worker process. They communicate only through Redis, making the system horizontally scalable.
Workers always check queues in this order before moving to the next. Critical jobs are always processed first, even if lower-priority jobs arrived earlier.
Priority Order: critical → high → default → low
Core language — goroutines power the worker pool
Lightweight idiomatic router, great for REST APIs
Powers the live dashboard — pushes job events to browser
Reads config from env vars and config.yaml
Structured JSON logging — essential for production
Unit tests for queue logic, handler tests
One command local setup: docker compose up
Runs tests + build on every push to main
Free tier, supports persistent Redis addon
A clean, idiomatic Go project layout. Each package has a single responsibility.
│ ├── server/ # main.go — starts HTTP API + dashboard
│ │ └── runner.go # executes job, handles panic recovery
│ ├── server.go # WebSocket hub + HTTP file server
Every piece of work in the system is represented as a Job. It is serialised to JSON and stored in Redis.
The pool starts N goroutines at startup. Each goroutine runs an infinite loop, blocking on Redis BRPOP until a job arrives. Graceful shutdown is handled via context cancellation.
p.wg.Wait() // wait for all in-flight jobs to finish
Instead of a single BRPOP, we poll queues in order. Redis LLEN checks each queue before popping, ensuring critical jobs are never starved by lower priorities.
// BRPOP blocks until any key has data, respecting order
if err != nil { return nil, err }
On failure, the job is not immediately moved to the DLQ. Instead, it is re-queued after a delay that doubles with each attempt. Only after MaxRetries failures does it move to the DLQ.
func Handle(ctx context.Context, job *Job, q queue.Queue, err error) {
Four key metrics give full visibility into queue health. These are exposed at GET /metrics and can be scraped by any Prometheus instance.
Help: "Total jobs pushed to the queue",
Help: "Time taken to process each job",
Help: "Total jobs moved to Dead Letter Queue",
The API server exposes endpoints to submit jobs, check status, and inspect the DLQ. All responses are JSON.
Submit a new job to the queue
Get job status and details by ID
List all jobs in the Dead Letter Queue
Re-queue a failed DLQ job manually
Health check — returns 200 OK + Redis ping
Register handlers at startup. Each handler receives a Job and returns an error. Three real handlers are included to show recruiters actual use cases, not just a toy example.
type HandlerFunc func(ctx context.Context, job *queue.Job) error
Uses Go's net/smtp to send a real email. Configure SMTP credentials via environment variables. In tests, mock the SMTP client.
POSTs the job payload as JSON to an external URL. Shows HTTP client usage with timeouts.
Downloads an image URL, resizes it using the golang.org/x/image package, and saves the output. A great demo of CPU-bound work in a background queue.
draw.BiLinear.Scale(dst, dst.Bounds(), src, src.Bounds(), draw.Over, nil)
The dashboard is a single HTML file served by the Go server itself. It connects to the /ws WebSocket endpoint and receives events for every job state change. No React, no npm — pure vanilla JS.
Live queue depth per priority (auto-updating bars)
Recent jobs table: ID, type, priority, status, duration
DLQ count with one-click retry button
Worker heartbeat — shows how many workers are active
client.send <- msg // fan out to all connected browsers
Follow this plan day by day. Always have something working at the end of each day — never leave the project in a broken state overnight.
go mod init, Redis connection, Job struct, basic LPUSH/BRPOP proof of concept
Priority queues (4 levels), Push(), Pop(), UpdateStatus(), Stats()
WorkerPool with N goroutines, graceful shutdown via context, handler registry
SendEmail, CallWebhook, ResizeImage — each handler tested in isolation
Exponential backoff, MaxRetries, MoveToDLQ(), GetDLQ(), re-queue endpoint
Failed jobs land in DLQ correctly
Chi router, all endpoints from Section 7, JSON responses, error handling
All API endpoints returning correct JSON
Input validation, request IDs, structured logging with zerolog, /health check
Prometheus counters + histograms, /metrics endpoint, verify with curl
WebSocket hub, broadcast job events, broadcast stats every 2s
HTML/CSS dashboard UI, live table, queue depth bars, DLQ retry button
docker compose up brings full stack
Deploy to Fly.io, record GIF demo, write README with architecture diagram
Day 12 tip: Use VHS (github.com/charmbracelet/vhs) or Kap to record a GIF of the dashboard. A GIF in the README gets 10x more recruiter attention than a text description.
# Scale workers: docker compose up --scale worker=3
flyctl launch # auto-detects Dockerfile, creates fly.toml
# Add free Redis via Upstash addon
Aim for solid coverage of the core queue and retry logic. Do not chase 100% coverage — focus on the business-critical paths that recruiters care about.
queue.Push() and queue.Pop() with a real Redis test instance (use testcontainers-go)
Priority ordering — push critical + low, verify critical pops first
Retry logic — simulate handler failure, assert retry count increments
DLQ — exceed MaxRetries, assert job lands in DLQ
Handler unit tests — mock external services (SMTP, HTTP) with interfaces
API integration tests — httptest.NewRecorder() for all endpoints
Your README is the first thing a recruiter sees. Treat it like a product landing page, not documentation.
Header: Project name + one-line description + badges (build passing, Go version, license)
GIF or screenshot of the live dashboard (3-5 seconds, auto-plays in GitHub)
Features list: 4 bullet points, each with an emoji
Architecture diagram (a simple ASCII or draw.io PNG is fine)
Quick start: docker compose up — get it running in one command
Tech stack badges at the bottom
Add a "Tech Decisions" section explaining WHY you chose Redis over RabbitMQ, WHY chi over Gin, etc. This shows engineering maturity and gives interviewers great questions to ask you.
14. How to Write This on Your Resume
Do not just list the project name. Write bullets that show impact and decisions, using the problem-action-result format.
GORO — Distributed Task Queue | Go, Redis, Docker, Prometheus | github.com/you/goro
• Built a concurrent worker pool handling 1,000+ jobs/min using Go goroutines
and channels, with priority scheduling across 4 queue levels
• Designed exponential backoff retry (1s → 16s) with Dead Letter Queue,
reducing job loss to zero in failure scenarios
• Exposed Prometheus /metrics endpoint tracking job throughput, latency
p50/p95, and DLQ depth — enabling full observability
• Built a real-time WebSocket dashboard served by Go itself, broadcasting
live job events to all connected clients with fan-out pattern
• Deployed to Fly.io with Docker multi-stage builds and GitHub Actions CI;
docker compose up brings full stack (app + Redis + Prometheus) in <30s
Why Redis over RabbitMQ or Kafka for this use case?
How does your priority queue prevent starvation of low-priority jobs?
What happens if the worker crashes mid-job? How do you handle at-least-once delivery?
How would you scale this to handle 100x more jobs?
Why exponential backoff? What are the tradeoffs vs fixed interval retry?
Walk me through the WebSocket fan-out pattern in your dashboard.

Pro tip: Answer the starvation question before they ask it. Add a note in your README: "Low-priority jobs are guaranteed to run within X minutes via a starvation check." This shows you thought about edge cases.
Go standard library — net/http, sync, context: pkg.go.dev/std
github.com/hibiken/asynq — production Go task queue (study the design, do not copy)
github.com/gocraft/work — older Go queue, good for understanding patterns
fly.io — free tier, supports TCP + HTTP, great WebSocket support
railway.app — free tier with Redis addon, easiest to set up
render.com — free tier, good for always-on services
github.com/testcontainers/testcontainers-go — spin up real Redis in tests
net/http/httptest — built-in Go package for API testing
Built with ❤️ in Go • Good luck with the project!
A phase-by-phase breakdown of everything to build, in order. Each phase ends with a working, testable milestone.
Create GitHub repo: goro, add MIT license + .gitignore for Go
Install Go 1.22+, Docker Desktop, Redis (local or via Docker)
Scaffold folder layout: cmd/server, cmd/worker, internal/ subdirs, config/
Run: go mod init github.com/you/goro — add all dependencies upfront
Read Section 2 (Architecture) and Section 5 (Data Structures) end to end
Goal: Redis talking to Go. Jobs moving through all 4 priority queues.
struct with all fields (ID, Type, Payload, Priority, Status, Retries, MaxRetries, timestamps)
Define Priority and Status constants; write priority helper (string → Redis key)
interface + RedisQueue: Push(), Pop(), GetJob(), UpdateStatus(), Stats()
Priority drain order: critical → high → default → low using ordered BRPop
Store job metadata in Redis HSET (key: job:{id}) alongside queue entry
✅ Milestone: Push 10 jobs across 4 priorities, pop them in correct order via
Goal: N goroutines concurrently consuming jobs with graceful shutdown.
struct: Start(ctx), runWorker(ctx), Stop() with sync.WaitGroup
Implement runner.Execute() with panic recovery — a panicking job must never kill the pool
Hook up handler registry: Register(jobType, fn) and Get(jobType)
Wire cmd/worker/main.go: read WORKER_COUNT from env, start pool, trap SIGTERM → cancel ctx → Stop()
✅ Milestone: 3 workers consuming jobs concurrently, Ctrl-C shuts down cleanly
Goal: 3 real job types executing, each unit-tested in isolation.
handlers/email.go — SendEmail via net/smtp; mock SMTP with an interface for tests
handlers/webhook.go — HTTP POST with 10 s timeout; return error on 4xx/5xx
handlers/resize.go — download image URL, BiLinear scale, save JPEG output
Register all 3 in cmd/worker/main.go; verify each fires via a manual Push test
✅ Milestone: All 3 handlers execute end-to-end + pass unit tests
🔄 Phase 4 — Retry Logic + Dead Letter Queue (Day 5)
Goal: Failed jobs retry with backoff and eventually land in DLQ.
retry/retry.go — Handle(): increment Retries, compute delay = 2^retries seconds
If Retries ≥ MaxRetries: MoveToDLQ(), set status = dlq, stop retrying
Add MoveToDLQ() and GetDLQ() to RedisQueue implementation
Wire retry.Handle() into runner.Execute() on non-nil error return
✅ Milestone: Set MaxRetries=2, make handler fail — job lands in DLQ after 3 attempts
Goal: All 9 API endpoints live, returning correct JSON, with validation and logging.
Day 6: Set up Chi router; wire POST /jobs, GET /jobs/:id, GET /queue/stats, GET /health
Day 6: Add GET /dlq and DELETE /dlq/:id/retry (re-queue from DLQ)
Day 7: Input validation (required fields, priority enum check), structured zerolog middleware
Day 7: Request IDs via middleware, consistent error envelope {error, code}, /health pings Redis
✅ Milestone: curl through every endpoint, every one returns correct status + JSON
Goal: /metrics endpoint serving live Prometheus data scrapers can consume.
Define 4 metrics: JobsEnqueued (counter), JobsProcessed (counter), JobDuration (histogram), DLQCounter (counter)
Instrument queue.Push() → Inc JobsEnqueued; runner.Execute() → Observe duration + Inc JobsProcessed on success
Mount promhttp.Handler() at GET /metrics in Chi router
Also track RetryCounter — increment in retry.Handle() before re-queuing
✅ Milestone: curl localhost:8080/metrics shows goro_jobs_enqueued_total and goro_job_duration_seconds
Goal: Live browser dashboard receiving job events via WebSocket.
Day 9: Build WebSocket Hub struct (clients map, broadcast channel, RWMutex, Run() goroutine)
Day 9: Emit job_update WSEvent from runner.Execute() on status change; stats ticker every 2 s
Day 10: HTML/CSS UI — queue depth bars, jobs table (ID, type, priority, status, duration), DLQ count + retry button
Day 10: Serve dashboard/static/index.html via http.FileServer; wire GET /ws upgrade
✅ Milestone: Open dashboard in browser, submit jobs via curl, see table update live
Goal: docker compose up brings the entire stack in one command. CI green on push.
Write multi-stage Dockerfile: golang:1.22-alpine builder → alpine:3.19 runtime; build both binaries
Write docker-compose.yml: redis + server + worker + prometheus services with correct env vars
Write .github/workflows/ci.yml: checkout + setup-go + redis service + go test ./... -race -cover + go build
✅ Milestone: docker compose up starts full stack; GitHub Actions badge shows passing
Goal: Live URL, GIF demo, polished README — project is recruiter-ready.
Deploy to Fly.io: flyctl launch, add Upstash Redis addon, set secrets, flyctl deploy
Record 5–8 s GIF of dashboard using VHS or Kap; commit to repo root as demo.gif
Write README: GIF at top, features, architecture diagram, quick start, API table, live demo link, tech stack badges
Add “Tech Decisions” section to README: why Redis, why Chi, why goroutines over a thread pool
Update resume bullets (see Section 14) with real numbers from your live deploy
✅ Milestone: Live URL works, GIF plays in README, CI is green — you’re done
Job struct, Queue interface, Redis round-trip
WebSocket hub + live HTML UI, DLQ retry button
Live URL, GIF demo, polished README — done
